title: Increased reproductive success of women after prenatal undernutrition.
ehr_evidence: birth records have been kept at the Wilhelmina Gasthuis, Amsterdam The study used medical birth records and interviews, not an electronic health record system. Participants were interviewed by telephone, visited at home, or visited at the hospital; physical measurements were taken using stadiometers and scales. No mention of EHR; data derived from a historical cohort at the Wilhelmina Gasthuis. None mentioned None; the study is a birth cohort study.
summary: A study investigating the effects of prenatal exposure to the Dutch famine (1944-1945) on the reproductive success of men and women.
key_findings: Women exposed to the Dutch famine in utero are more reproductively successful than those not exposed.
Exposed women have more offspring, more twins, are less likely to remain childless, and start reproducing at a younger age.
In utero exposure to famine did not affect the reproductive success of males.
The study aims to evaluate the effects of circumscribed maternal undernutrition on offspring's reproductive success and physical measurements.

F1 women exposed to famine in utero had more children (mean 2.0 vs 1.7), were younger at first child (23.2 vs 23.7 years), more frequently had twins (OR 2.7), and were less frequently childless (OR 0.80).
No significant alterations in reproductive success were found in F1 men.
F1 women exposed to famine in utero had more children (mean 2.0 vs 1.7), were younger at first birth, and had a higher frequency of twins.
F1 men exposed to famine in utero showed no significant alterations in reproductive success.
F1 women exposed to famine in utero had more offspring
In utero famine exposure is sufficient for the fertile phenotype.
No difference in reproductive success between F1 women born before or conceived after the famine.
Findings consistent with the theory of life history regulation regarding trade-offs between fertility and body maintenance.
population: Men and women born around 1944-1945 in Amsterdam, Netherlands
limitations: Participation bias (only 55% of eligible cohort participated)
Specific to a single hospital population
Unable to investigate whether fetal survival under harsh conditions played a role.
